Patch Set 1:

Onyx Autotest retesting enforced review-zfs-part-2 session. Requested by Jinshan Xiong because of LU-8550

Patch Set 1:

This is a blocker, please review this patch at your earliest convenience

Patch Set 1:

(1 comment)

Do we use mknod() to create volatile files? If so then why?

Patch Set 1:

> Do we use mknod() to create volatile files? If so then why?

We don't use mknod(). From what I have seen, libc open() can call mknod() to create new files for some reasons.

Patch Set 1:

> We don't use mknod(). From what I have seen, libc open() can call mknod() to create new files for some reasons.

Could you post the details?

Patch Set 1:

> Could you post the details?

I don't have details. I got this by checking lustre log file, and I don't know what's the libc version of affected systems. So far it could be seen on sles and centos6.8. sles is always weird. I couldn't reproduce this on centos7.2

Patch Set 1:

It's not libc open() that's calling mknod(). It must be ll_create_nd() calling ll_mknod().

Patch Set 1:

> It's not libc open() that's calling mknod(). It must be ll_create_nd() calling ll_mknod().

There should be messages printed in ll_create_nd().

Ah I realized HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN may not be defined on those systems.

Patch Set 1:

I don't see how this will work.

Patch Set 1:

Jinshan, HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN is #undef on el6 and sles11sp4.  it is #defined on el7.

Patch Set 1: Verified+1

Tests uploaded by Onyx Autotest from onyx-33vm1 for review-zfs-part-2 received by maloo, run on Red Hat Enterprise Linux Server 7.3/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/12da4b8c-ab93-11e6-a726-5254006e85c2). Ran 8 tests. No failures.
Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 1:

This will mean that MDT inodes for file created by mknod() with volatile names are leaked until orhpan cleanup (which is minor but is still a bug), since volatile file destruction depends on a close RPC being received. That's also why the patch works.

This does solve the bigger problem that without HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN volatile files are not always volatile (in the cases where ll_create_nd() is called without an intent).

Patch Set 1:

> This will mean that MDT inodes for file created by mknod() with volatile names are leaked until orhpan cleanup (which is minor but is still a bug), since volatile file destruction depends on a close RPC being received.

Yes, things are weird here. An orphan file is supposed to be an open-unlinked file, but now we have an inode under orphan directory w/o openers. If the MDT crashed during this period, the open following mknod() will fail because volatile file has been removed.

Patch Set 1:

the chance of this happening is really little though. Not sure if it worths some extra effort to fix it.

Patch Set 1:

not sure how it helps - ll_mknod() seems to be a wrapper of ll_new_node().

Patch Set 1:

From ll_create_nd() we would call ll_new_node(..., LUSTRE_OPC_CREATE) but from ll_mknod() we would call ll_new_node(..., LUSTRE_OPC_MKNOD).

Patch Set 1:

I can change it that way.

My question was how ll_new_node(, LUSTRE_OPC_CREATE) will fix the leaking problem because it's just a wrapper of ll_mknod().

Patch Set 1:

and my patch does it in the same way.

Patch Set 1:

let me rephrase this :-)

Calling ll_new_node() with LUSTRE_OPC_CREATE will fix the problem of identifying volatile file, which is identical to my current patch, but it won't fix the leaking problem.

I tend to think we should get rid of LUSTRE_OPC_MKNOD completely because it's not being used anywhere else.

parents.subject: LU-8824 nodemap: load nodemap definitions first

commit.subject: LU-8559 llite: fix ll_create_nd for non HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN
commit.message:
LU-8559 llite: fix ll_create_nd for non HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN

Invoke ll_new_node() with LUSTRE_OPC_CREATE for non
HAVE_IOP_ATOMIC_OPEN case so that it can recognize volatile
file name.

Signed-off-by: Jinshan Xiong <jinshan.xiong@intel.com>
Change-Id: Ia38d353844dc4852dbaa308fe26f450108a009ea
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/23758
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: wangdi <di.wang@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
